title: Listening - Part 2
description: You will hear an acoustics expert called Oliver Grant giving a talk about the impact of noise in modern cities. For questions 7 – 14, complete the sentences with a word or short phrase.
instructions: You will hear an acoustics expert called Oliver Grant giving a talk about the impact of noise in modern cities. For questions 7 – 14, complete the sentences with a word or short phrase.

extracts.context: Oliver Grant discusses how urban design affects noise levels and the health of city dwellers.
extracts.audio_script:
Good evening. I’m Oliver Grant. We live in an increasingly loud world, and my job is to figure out how to make it quieter. I’m an environmental acoustician. My interest in this field didn't start with a scientific journal; it actually began when I was living in a [7]small studio apartment[/7] next to a train line. I noticed that the noise didn't just bother my ears; it affected my mood and my ability to work. I wanted to understand why some sounds are merely annoying while others are genuinely harmful.

Most people think of noise as a simple nuisance, but it has profound effects on our biology. When we hear a sudden loud noise, our bodies release stress hormones like cortisol. I was surprised to find in my research that even [8]low-frequency hums[/8] from air conditioning units can lead to long-term health issues like hypertension. Our brains are evolved to listen for predators, so they never truly switch off, even when we are sleeping.

In urban planning, we have traditionally used 'noise walls'—those big concrete barriers you see next to motorways. However, we are finding that these are often ineffective. I’ve been experimenting with 'sonic crystals', which are essentially [9]cylindrical rods[/9] arranged in a specific pattern. They don't just block sound; they cancel out specific frequencies through interference. They look like modern art installations, but they are incredibly functional at reducing traffic noise in residential areas.

We also look at the materials used in building facades. The modern trend for glass and steel is a disaster for acoustics because these materials are highly reflective. I’ve noticed that the [10]surface texture[/10] of a building is the most important factor in whether it absorbs or scatters sound. By adding green walls or even subtle patterns to the concrete, we can significantly reduce the 'echo' effect that makes city streets feel so loud.

One of the most interesting projects I’ve worked on was in a city park. We wanted to create a 'quiet zone' without building walls. We found that the [11]sound of water[/11] from a large fountain was the most effective way to mask the surrounding traffic. It’s a psychological trick—the human brain prioritizes the natural, rhythmic sound of the fountain over the irregular noise of cars. It creates a 'perceived' quietness that is very relaxing for visitors.

Data collection is a big part of my job. We use hundreds of sensors to create 'noise maps' of cities. I’ve found that the [12]delivery of goods[/12] during the night is the biggest contributor to sleep disturbance in major capitals. It’s a difficult economic problem to solve, as businesses need their supplies, but the social cost in terms of public health is enormous.

Looking forward, I’m optimistic about the rise of electric vehicles. They are much quieter at low speeds, but I’m worried that the [13]tyre noise[/13] they produce at higher speeds will remain a problem. At speeds over 50 kilometers per hour, the friction of the rubber on the road is actually louder than the engine itself. We need to rethink our road surfaces to truly quieten our cities.

Finally, I’m currently writing a [14]report for Parliament[/14] on the future of urban acoustics. I hope it will lead to stricter building regulations that prioritize quietness as a basic human right. We need to start designing our cities for our ears, not just our eyes. Thank you for your time.

dialogue.speaker: oliver
